text: We usually start with the division of the pulmonary ligament.
Arm one is used to retract the right lower lobe forward and upward.
The inferior pulmonary vein is identified and protected.
A gauze rolled like a cigar is used with arm one to retract the right lower lobe forward.
This maneuver facilitates dissection of the posterior mediastinal pleura.
At this point, the subcarinal lymph nodes and mediastinal lymph node station 7 are excised.
It is common to encounter large bronchial arteries surrounding the lymph nodes which should be well cauterized.
This avoids a bloody field that could obscure identification of the vagus nerve and the esophagus and could put these structures at risk for injury.
Next, the sump node located between the right upper lobe bronchus and the bronchus intermedius is dissected to create the landing zone for future completion of the posterior oblique fissure.
The medial basilar pulmonary artery branch is a good landmark to dissect before completing the anterior oblique fissure.
The right middle lobe bronchus also must be identified and protected.
A vessel loop is used for retraction.
It helps to obtain a good angle for a robotic green load 30 millimeter stapler.
Next the pulmonary artery is dissected within the fissure.
The superior segmental arteries must be identified and protected.
The tip-up instrument in arm number one is used to roll the lung forward and a vessel loop is delivered to facilitate the creation of a comfortable window for the robotic stapler.
The posterior oblique fissure is then divided with a green load robotic stapler.
The right middle lobe artery is identified and protected.
The ongoing pulmonary artery for the right lower lobe is dissected free circumferentially.
My preference is to use blunt instruments to create a comfortable window around vessels to be divided.
Once that window has been created, the ongoing pulmonary artery is encircled with a vessel loop and divided with a wide-load 30mm robotic stapler.
Attention should be paid to avoid the staple line to occlude the blood flow into the right middle of artery.
Next, the cardiac instrument in arm number four is placed in between the inferior and superior pulmonary veins, and the TPAP instrument in arm number one is used to roll the lung forward.
The inferior pulmonary vein is dissected free circumferentially and circled with a vessel loop and divided with a wide-load 30mm robotic stapler.
The right middle lobe bronchus is identified and protected.
The right lower lobe bronchus is divided with a green load 30 millimeter robotic stapler.
My preference is to gently inflate the lung before firing the stapler to make sure that the staple line is not occluding air flow into the right middle lobe bronchus.
The specimen is removed using a thoracoscopic bag.
Mediastinal lymph node dissection is completed such as in this case at the remaining sump node.
My preference is to use the bipolar dissector for fine dissection around leaf nodes.
All visible subcarina leaf nodes are also removed.
Surgicel's node is used for hemostasis.
The right lower paratracheal leaf nodes at station 4R are also dissected.
Retraction with arm number two should be very well controlled during leaf node dissection.
This avoids tearing the leaf node and creating bleeding which would slow down the operation.
A finger of a sterile glove is used to remove large unfragable leaf nodes.
Xperel is injected into multiple intercostal spaces using a 25 gauge butterfly needle.
